set1.word: Ravi said, "I saw him last night."
options: She said she has seen him last night.
She said she had seen him the previous night.
She said she was seeing him last night.
She said she had saw him the previous night.
explanation: Backshift rule: the reporting verb 'said' is in the past tense, so every tense in the direct speech must shift one step back into the past. Simple Past 'saw' becomes Past Perfect 'had seen', because Simple Past always moves specifically to Past Perfect (there is no other past-of-past form). At the same time, the time word 'last night' must change to 'the previous night', since 'last night' is only accurate on the day right after the event; once reported later, a neutral phrase is needed. Both the tense and the time word must change together: option (a) skips the tense shift, option (c) wrongly uses Past Continuous, and option (d) uses the incorrect verb form 'had saw' instead of 'had seen'.

set1.word: She said, "I was very tired yesterday."
options: She said she had been very tired the day before.
She said she was very tired yesterday.
She said she had been very tired yesterday.
She said she is very tired the day before.
explanation: Simple Past 'was' backshifts to Past Perfect 'had been', since 'was' is the Simple Past of 'be' and always moves to Past Perfect under backshift. At the same time 'yesterday' becomes 'the day before', because 'yesterday' is only correct when spoken the very next day; once reported later, the neutral phrase 'the day before' is used instead. Both changes are required together: option (b) changes neither, option (c) shifts the tense but leaves 'yesterday' unchanged, and option (d) wrongly uses the present tense 'is'. Only the correct answer performs both shifts.

set1.word: He said, "I have seen that movie already."
options: He said that he saw that movie already.
He said that he had seen that movie already.
He said that he have seen that movie already.
He said that he was seeing that movie already.
explanation: This tests the Present Perfect to Past Perfect backshift: 'have seen' becomes 'had seen' because the reporting verb 'said' is past. A common trap is option (a), 'saw' (Simple Past) — students often confuse Present Perfect with Simple Past since both describe completed actions, but grammatically Present Perfect must always backshift specifically to Past Perfect, never to Simple Past. Option (c) fails to change the auxiliary 'have' to 'had' at all, and option (d) wrongly turns it into Past Continuous, changing a completed action into an ongoing one.

set1.word: Rohit said, "I had completed the project."
options: Rohit said he has completed the project.
Rohit said he completed the project.
Rohit said that he had completed the project.
Rohit said that he was completing the project.
explanation: This tests an important exception to the backshift rule: Past Perfect is already the 'furthest back' past tense in English, so it has nowhere further to shift — it simply stays as 'had completed'. Many students assume every tense must change and wrongly pick option (a) or (b), but reducing Past Perfect to Present Perfect or Simple Past would actually distort the timeline and change the meaning. Option (d) is wrong because it turns a completed action into an ongoing one. Remember: once a sentence is already in Past Perfect, indirect speech leaves it exactly as it is.

set1.word: He said, "I am reading a book."
options: He said he is reading a book.
He said that he was reading a book.
He said that he had read a book.
He said he read a book.
explanation: Present Continuous 'am reading' backshifts to Past Continuous 'was reading' — one of the most common transformations in Narration. Option (a) fails to backshift the tense at all. Option (c) wrongly jumps to Past Perfect, turning an ongoing action into a completed one, and option (d) incorrectly uses Simple Past, losing the 'in progress' sense that the continuous aspect conveys. Always pair Present Continuous with Past Continuous, keeping the '-ing' form and only changing 'am/is/are' to 'was/were'.

set1.word: She said, "I was reading a novel at that time."
options: She said she had been reading a novel at that time.
She said she read a novel at that time.
She said she had read a novel at that time.
She said she was reading a novel then.
explanation: Past Continuous 'was reading' backshifts one further step to Past Perfect Continuous 'had been reading', because the reporting verb is past and the original quote was already in a past tense — this is different from a Present Continuous original, which only shifts one step to Past Continuous. Option (b) drops the continuous sense entirely. Option (c) wrongly treats it as a single completed action instead of an ongoing one. Option (d) fails to backshift the tense at all, only changing the time phrase from 'at that time' to 'then'.

set1.word: The scientist said, "We have been working on this project for months."
options: The scientist said they had been working on that project for months.
The scientist said they have been working on this project for months.
The scientist said that they were working on that project.
The scientist said that we had worked on this project.
explanation: Present Perfect Continuous 'have been working' backshifts to Past Perfect Continuous 'had been working'. Alongside the verb, two other standard shifts occur: the pronoun 'We' becomes 'they' (the scientist's words are now being reported by someone else, not quoted by the scientist), and 'this' becomes 'that' (something described as near at the time of speaking becomes distant once reported later). Option (b) fails to backshift the tense, option (c) loses the ongoing, continuous sense of the action, and option (d) wrongly keeps 'We', which would only be correct if the scientist were narrating in first person.

set1.word: She told her friend that she had been studying for her examination. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "I had studied for my examination."
"I am studying for my examination."
"I was studying for my examination."
"I have been studying for my examination."
explanation: This is a reverse question — converting Indirect speech back into Direct speech — so every backshifted tense must be shifted forward again. 'had been studying' (Past Perfect Continuous) is the backshifted form of Past Continuous 'was studying', so reversing it gives 'was studying'. Although Present Perfect Continuous 'have been studying' could theoretically also backshift to 'had been studying', when reversing indirect speech to direct speech, the simplest matching form — Past Continuous — is treated as the standard exam answer. Option (a) wrongly drops the continuous aspect, and options (b) and (d) fail to move the tense back into the past, which contradicts 'told' being a past reporting verb.

set1.word: He said, "The sun rises in the east."
options: He said that the sun rose in the east.
He said that the sun has risen in the east.
He said that the sun rises in the east.
He said that the sun had risen in the east.
explanation: This introduces one of the most important exceptions to the backshift rule: Universal Truths and scientific facts never change tense in indirect speech, no matter what tense the reporting verb is in. 'The sun rises in the east' is a permanent fact of nature, so it stays in the Present Tense — 'rises' — even though 'said' is past. Options (a), (b) and (d) all incorrectly apply the normal backshift rule, which does not apply here. Whenever a general truth, scientific law, or proverb appears inside quotation marks, its tense stays untouched.

set1.word: The geologist explained that tectonic shifts were inevitable.
options: The geologist said, "Tectonic shifts are inevitable."
"Tectonic shifts were inevitable," said the geologist.
The geologist explained, "Tectonic shifts will occur."
"Inevitably, tectonic shifts occur," he said.
explanation: This is another Universal Truth question, presented in reverse (Indirect to Direct) and worded as a general scientific statement, which is treated as a timeless fact. Even though the indirect version uses the past tense 'were' (because 'explained' is a past reporting verb), the correct Direct Speech restores the Present Tense — 'are inevitable' — since universal or general truths keep the present tense in their original quoted form. Option (b) wrongly keeps 'were' straight from the indirect sentence, option (c) changes the meaning into a future prediction, and option (d) rewords the sentence instead of accurately reconstructing what was actually said.

set2.word: The scientist stated that water boiled at 100 degrees Celsius.
options: "Water will boil at 100 degrees Celsius," said the scientist.
"Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius," the scientist stated.
"Water had boiled at 100 degrees Celsius," said the scientist.
"Water is boiling at 100 degrees Celsius," said the scientist.
explanation: Scientific facts are Universal Truths, so regardless of the past tense 'boiled' appearing in the indirect sentence, the original Direct Speech must be restored to the Simple Present Tense — 'boils' — because this is a permanent property of water, not a one-time past event. Option (a) wrongly turns it into a future prediction, option (c) makes it sound like a completed past event rather than a fact, and option (d) incorrectly uses Present Continuous, as if the boiling were happening right now instead of describing a general truth.

set2.word: Mrs. Gomes said, "Virtue is its own reward."
options: Mrs. Gomes said that Virtue was its own reward.
Mrs. Gomes said that Virtue is its own reward.
Mrs. Gomes said that Virtue had its own reward.
Mrs. Gomes said that Virtue has its own reward.
explanation: 'Virtue is its own reward' is a well-known moral proverb, and like scientific facts, proverbs and universally accepted truths keep their Present Tense in indirect speech even after a past reporting verb. Option (a) incorrectly backshifts to the past tense, option (c) changes the meaning entirely (as if virtue 'possessed' a reward in the past), and option (d) wrongly uses Present Perfect. The safe rule: any timeless statement of wisdom or morality is exempt from backshifting.

set2.word: The teacher said, "William Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon."
options: ...have been born...
...was born...
...has been born...
...is born...
explanation: This is a classic trap testing the difference between a Universal Truth and a Historical Fact. A Universal Truth is permanently true regardless of time (like 'the sun rises in the east'), but a Historical Fact refers to a specific dated event that already happened — it is not exempt from the past tense, because it describes something that occurred at a fixed point in history rather than something generally true at all times. Since 'Shakespeare was born...' describes a completed historical event, it stays in the Past Tense — 'was born' — and does not shift to present, unlike a universal truth. Options (a), (c) and (d) all incorrectly try to apply an exemption that only applies to genuinely timeless statements, not historical facts.

options: He said I must work hard.
He said that I had to work hard.
He said that you must work hard.
He said that I have to work hard.
explanation: The modal 'must' has two possible meanings, and its transformation in indirect speech depends on which meaning is intended. When 'must' expresses obligation, compulsion, or an order (as here, telling someone they 'must work hard'), it backshifts to 'had to' in indirect speech. If instead 'must' expressed logical certainty or a permanent rule, it would stay unchanged. Since this sentence is clearly an instruction, 'must' correctly becomes 'had to'. Option (a) fails to change the pronoun 'You' to match the reported subject, option (c) leaves 'must' unchanged (wrong for the obligation sense), and option (d) wrongly uses the present-tense 'have to'.

set2.word: They said, "We shall overcome this challenge together."
options: They said they will overcome that challenge together.
They said they should overcome this challenge together.
They said they would overcome that challenge together.
They said they shall overcome that challenge together.
explanation: The modal 'shall' (used here for simple future, first person) backshifts to 'would', following the same pattern as 'will' to 'would'. At the same time, 'this' (close, at the time of speaking) becomes 'that' (more distant, once reported later) — the standard demonstrative-pronoun shift, parallel to 'these' becoming 'those'. Option (a) incorrectly uses 'will' instead of 'would', option (b) wrongly turns it into an obligation with 'should', and option (d) fails to backshift 'shall' to 'would', even though it correctly fixes the demonstrative pronoun.

set2.word: He promised, "He will not do the mistake again."
options: He promised that he would not do the mistake next time.
He promised that he would not did the mistake next time.
He promised that he will not do the mistake again.
He promises that he will not do the mistake again.
explanation: With the past reporting verb 'promised', 'will' backshifts to 'would', following the regular future-to-conditional pattern. At the same time, the time word 'again' — meaning 'another time in the future' relative to the promise — is converted to 'next time' once the promise is being reported afterward. Option (b) uses the ungrammatical double-past form 'would not did'. Option (c) fails to backshift 'will' at all. Option (d) wrongly keeps the reporting verb itself in the present tense ('promises'), which contradicts the sentence clearly describing something that happened in the past ('He promised').

set2.word: He mentioned that his team would start the project next week. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: He mentioned, "My team will start the project next week."
He mentioned, "My team is starting the project next week."
He mentioned, "My team would be starting the project next week."
He said, "The project shall be started next week."
explanation: This reverse question undoes the 'will to would' backshift, so 'would start' correctly becomes 'will start' in Direct Speech. Notice that 'next week' stays the same in both versions; unlike 'today' or 'tomorrow', which shift depending on when the reporting happens, 'next week' is a fixed reference to a point after the moment of speaking and generally does not change on its own. Also, 'his team' correctly becomes 'My team', since the person being quoted is now speaking about their own team. Options (b) and (c) incorrectly change the tense or aspect, and option (d) changes the meaning through the passive voice and the modal 'shall' instead of 'will'.

set2.word: He said, "Would you help me with this work?"
options: He said if I would help him with that work.
He asked if I would help him with that work.
He asked would I help him with this work.
He told me would I help him.
explanation: Two rules combine here. First, 'would' is already a backshifted, conditional modal form, so it does not change further in indirect speech — it simply stays 'would'. Second, since the direct sentence is a Yes/No question (starting with a modal verb, 'Would you...?'), the reporting verb must be 'asked', and the question is connected using 'if', with no inversion — meaning the subject comes before the verb, as in a normal statement ('if I would help'), not 'would I help'. Option (a) uses the wrong reporting verb 'said' instead of 'asked' for a question, and options (c) and (d) both wrongly keep the inverted, question-style word order, a very commonly tested mistake.

set2.word: Mona said to me, "Are you planning a trip to Disneyland?"
options: Mona enquired of me if I was planning a trip to Disneyland.
Mona asked me that was I planning a trip to Disneyland?
Mona enquired of me that was I planning a trip to Disneyland.
Mona asked me if I had been planning a trip to Disneyland?
explanation: This is another Yes/No question, so it must be converted using 'if' or 'whether' with no inversion. The correct pattern is 'enquired of me if I was planning...' — subject 'I' comes before the verb 'was', exactly like a normal statement. Options (b) and (c) both incorrectly keep the inverted question form 'was I' and also wrongly mix 'that' with a question — 'that' should never introduce a Yes/No question; only 'if' or 'whether' can. Option (d) wrongly over-backshifts Present Continuous all the way to Past Perfect Continuous, when it should only move one step to Past Continuous.

set2.word: She said to herself, "Shall I have a happy married life?"
options: She wondered if she shall have a happy married life.
She asked that if she should have a happy married life.
She wondered if she would have a happy married life.
She thought that she should have a happy married life.
explanation: When someone asks a question 'to themselves' (self-talk, shown here by 'said to herself'), the reporting verb changes to 'wondered' or 'thought' rather than 'asked', since there is no one else present to be asked. This is a Yes/No question, so it needs 'if' or 'whether' with no inversion. The modal 'shall' (asking about future possibility) backshifts to 'would', the same rule seen earlier in this set. Option (a) fails to backshift 'shall'. Option (b) wrongly combines 'that' and 'if' together (never do this) and also wrongly uses 'should' for a future-possibility meaning. Option (d) drops the question-marker 'if' entirely, turning it into a plain statement.

set3.word: She said to me, "Can you look after my baby for a short while?"
options: She asked me that if I could look after her baby for a short while.
She asked me if I can look after my baby for a short while.
She asked me if you can look after my baby for a short while.
She asked me if I could look after her baby for a short while.
explanation: This question tests the pronoun-shift rule together with the modal backshift 'can' to 'could'. Since 'my baby' belongs to the speaker, once the sentence is reported by the listener ('me'), 'my' must change to 'her', because from the reporter's point of view the baby belongs to someone else, not to themselves. Similarly, 'you' (the listener, 'me') changes to 'I', since the listener is now the one doing the reporting. Option (a) wrongly combines 'that' with 'if' (redundant and incorrect together), option (b) fails to backshift 'can' to 'could' and also fails to change 'my' to 'her', and option (c) incorrectly keeps 'you' instead of changing it to 'I'.

set3.word: The customer asked the tailor, "Will you have the dress ready by Tuesday?"
options: if he should have the dress ready by Tuesday.
if he would have the dress ready by Tuesday.
if he will have the dress ready by Tuesday.
if he would like to have the dress ready by Tuesday.
explanation: A standard Yes/No question conversion: 'will' backshifts to 'would' (the tailor is referred to as 'he', since the customer is speaking about someone else), connected using 'if'. Option (a) wrongly uses 'should' (a different modal expressing obligation, not future), option (c) fails to backshift 'will' to 'would', and option (d) adds an unnecessary rewording ('would like to') that changes the original plain future question into a request.

options: He asked what are you doing.
He asked what I had been doing.
He asked what was I doing.
He asked what I was doing.
explanation: This is the single most commonly tested trap in Wh-question Narration: even though the direct sentence is a question, the indirect version must follow normal statement word order — subject before verb ('what I was doing') — never question word order ('what was I doing'). Wh-questions keep the question word itself (what, where, why, who) instead of adding 'if/whether', but the inversion of subject and auxiliary must always be removed. Option (a) keeps full inversion and fails to backshift the tense. Option (c) correctly backshifts to Past Continuous but still wrongly keeps 'was I doing'. Option (b) wrongly over-backshifts to Past Perfect Continuous. Only the correct answer gets both the tense and word order right.

set3.word: He said, "How long have you been waiting?"
options: He asked how long had I been waiting.
He said how long I was waiting.
He asked how long I have been waiting.
He asked how long I had been waiting.
explanation: Present Perfect Continuous 'have been waiting' backshifts to Past Perfect Continuous 'had been waiting', the correct one-step-further backshift for this tense. As with all Wh-questions, normal statement word order applies: 'how long I had been waiting', not 'how long had I been waiting'. Option (a) keeps the incorrect inverted order. Option (b) wrongly uses the reporting verb 'said' instead of 'asked' and only partially backshifts the tense (Past Continuous instead of Past Perfect Continuous). Option (c) fails to backshift the tense at all.

options: Teacher asked who had broken the window.
Teacher asked who has broken the window.
Teacher asked who did break the window.
Teacher said who broke the window.
explanation: This tests a special rule for Wh-questions where the question word itself ('Who') is the subject of the sentence — in such cases, no auxiliary verb like 'did' or 'does' is inserted, because there is nothing to invert in the first place ('Who broke' is already in normal statement order). Simple Past 'broke' backshifts to Past Perfect 'had broken'. Option (b) fails to backshift the tense. Option (c) incorrectly inserts 'did', which is not needed when 'who' is the subject. Option (d) wrongly keeps the reporting verb 'said' for a question (should be 'asked') and also fails to backshift the tense.

set3.word: She said, "Why didn't you tell me the truth earlier?"
options: She asked why I hadn't told her the truth earlier.
She asked why hadn't I told her the truth earlier.
She said why I didn't tell her the truth earlier.
She questioned why did I not tell her the truth.
explanation: Negative Simple Past 'didn't tell' backshifts to negative Past Perfect 'hadn't told', following the same logic as positive sentences. As always with Wh-questions, statement word order is required — 'why I hadn't told her', not 'why hadn't I told her', which wrongly keeps the inverted question form. Option (b) makes exactly this inversion error. Option (c) uses correct word order but fails to backshift the tense at all. Option (d) both keeps the inverted 'did I not' structure and drops the required time phrase 'earlier'.

set3.word: The teacher was happy to meet her and asked, "When did you complete all these?"
options: ...asked when she had completed all those.
...asked when she had been completing all those.
...asked when did she complete all those.
...asked when had she completed all those.
explanation: Simple Past 'did...complete' backshifts to Past Perfect 'had completed', and the demonstrative 'these' (near, at the time of speaking) shifts to 'those' (more distant, once reported later) — the same this/that, these/those pattern seen throughout Narration. As always with Wh-questions, no inversion is allowed: 'when she had completed', not 'when did she complete' (option c) or 'when had she completed' (option d), both of which wrongly retain question-style inversion. Option (b) unnecessarily changes the tense to Past Perfect Continuous, which is not needed since the original describes a single completed action, not an ongoing one.

set3.word: The teacher asked me why I had reached the examination hall so late. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "Why had I reached the examination hall so late?"
"Why did you reach the examination hall so late?"
"Why you reached the examination hall so late?"
"Why are you reaching the examination hall so late?"
explanation: This reverse question undoes the tense backshift and restores natural question word order. 'had reached' (Past Perfect) reverses to 'did reach' (Simple Past interrogative), and — because this is now a real question being asked aloud in Direct Speech — the auxiliary 'did' must come before the subject: 'did you reach' (inversion is restored, the opposite of what happens going from Direct to Indirect). Also, 'I' (the student, referred to by the teacher) becomes 'you' in Direct Speech, since the teacher is speaking directly to the student. Option (a) wrongly keeps Past Perfect without inversion. Option (c) has no auxiliary 'did' at all, which is ungrammatical for a direct question. Option (d) wrongly uses Present Continuous instead of Simple Past.

set3.word: The teacher said, "Be quiet in the class."
options: The teacher told be quiet in the class.
The teacher told them to be quiet in the class.
The teacher said them to be quiet in the class.
The teacher told them to quiet in the class.
explanation: This tests converting an Imperative (a command) into indirect speech. The standard pattern is: reporting verb 'told' (never 'said' when an object follows) plus the object (the person commanded, here 'them') plus 'to' plus the base form of the verb — so 'Be quiet' becomes 'told them to be quiet'. Option (a) is missing the object entirely. Option (c) wrongly uses 'said' with a direct object — 'said' cannot take a personal object like this; only 'told' can. Option (d) breaks the infinitive by dropping 'be', leaving the invalid phrase 'to quiet'.

options: He said do it immediately.
He told to do it now.
He told me to do it at once.
He told me do it right now.
explanation: Another Imperative-to-Indirect conversion, following the pattern 'told + object + to + verb'. Here 'me' is the object (the person receiving the order), correctly included as 'told me to do it'. Additionally, 'right now' is converted to the more formal reported phrase 'at once'. Option (a) does not attempt the correct structure and simply keeps 'said' unchanged. Option (b) is missing the object 'me' ('told to do' is incomplete — 'told' always needs an object). Option (d) includes the object 'me' but is missing the essential 'to' before the verb, and it also fails to change the time phrase.

options: Rita asked to close the window.
Rita said to close the window.
Rita requested that the window is closed.
Rita requested to close the window.
explanation: When the direct sentence contains the word 'Please', it signals a polite request rather than a plain command, so the reporting verb changes to 'requested' instead of 'told' or 'ordered'. A fully complete sentence would ideally also include an object ('requested me to close the window'), but this exam's official answer key accepts the object-less form 'requested to close the window' as standard here — a pattern worth remembering, since it can appear this way in the actual exam. Option (a) uses 'asked' instead of 'requested', missing the politeness signalled by 'Please'. Option (b) does not reflect any change in the reporting verb. Option (c) incorrectly uses a 'that'-clause with an unchanged present-tense verb, which is not the pattern used for request-type imperatives.

set4.word: He said to me, "Please pass the salt."
options: He asked for the salt.
He requested me to pass the salt.
He requested me that I pass the salt.
He advised me that I should pass the salt.
explanation: This shows the fully standard, textbook pattern for polite requests: 'requested' plus object ('me') plus 'to' plus the base verb ('to pass'). Compare this with the previous question in this set, where the object was omitted in the official answer — here the object is included, which is the pattern you should generally follow unless a specific answer key says otherwise. Option (a) changes the meaning completely, turning a request to 'pass' the salt into a request 'for' the salt. Option (c) incorrectly mixes 'requested' with a 'that'-clause instead of the infinitive pattern. Option (d) wrongly changes the reporting verb to 'advised', which implies giving advice rather than making a simple polite request.

set4.word: The boss said to the secretary, "Don't forget to order A3 size paper."
options: The boss instructed the secretary not to forget to order A3 size paper.
The boss told the secretary that she didn't forget to order A3 size paper.
The boss asked the secretary to order A3 size paper.
The boss told the secretary that she should not forget to order A3 size paper.
explanation: Negative Imperatives ('Don't...') are converted using the pattern: reporting verb plus object plus 'not to' plus base verb. Here, 'Don't forget' correctly becomes 'not to forget', with 'instructed' as an appropriate reporting verb for a boss giving a directive to a secretary. Option (b) incorrectly uses a 'that'-clause with simple past tense, losing the instructional force of the original. Option (c) oversimplifies the meaning by dropping 'Don't forget' and just saying 'order', losing the reminder aspect of the sentence. Option (d) unnecessarily complicates the sentence with 'should not', when the simple infinitive pattern ('not to forget') is the standard, natural way to report negative commands.

set4.word: Mudassir warned the child, "Be careful and be slow."
options: ...that he be careful and be slow.
...to be careful and to be slow.
...to be careful and should be slow.
...that he should be careful and be slow.
explanation: When two commands are joined in one imperative sentence ('Be careful and be slow'), each command must be converted using its own 'to' plus base verb in indirect speech, keeping parallel structure: 'to be careful and to be slow'. Repeating 'to' before the second verb, rather than dropping it, is essential for grammatical parallelism. Option (a) does not use the infinitive form at all and awkwardly repeats 'that he be'. Option (c) mixes two different structures — 'to be careful' (infinitive) and 'should be slow' (modal) — breaking the required parallelism. Option (d) similarly mixes 'that he should' with a bare 'be slow', another parallel-structure violation.

options: I said let us start early.
I told that we must start early.
I suggested that we should start early.
I suggested us to start early.
explanation: 'Let us' (used to propose doing something together) has a fixed, special conversion pattern in indirect speech: reporting verb 'suggested' plus 'that' plus subject plus 'should' plus the base verb. This differs from ordinary imperatives, which use a 'to'-infinitive — 'Let us' sentences never take the infinitive form. Option (a) makes no transformation. Option (b) wrongly uses 'told...must', the pattern for strong obligation or orders, not suggestions. Option (d) incorrectly uses the infinitive pattern 'suggested us to start', which is grammatically wrong — 'suggest' cannot be followed directly by 'object + to-infinitive' in standard English; it needs a 'that'-clause.

set4.word: She said, "Let him not be disturbed during the meeting."
options: She asked not to disturb him during the meeting.
She advised that he should not be disturbed during the meeting.
She said that let him not be disturbed.
She ordered no one disturb him.
explanation: 'Let him not...' expresses a suggestion or piece of advice about a third person (unlike 'let us', which is about the speaker and listener together). This follows the pattern: 'advised/suggested that + subject + should not + base verb' — here, 'advised that he should not be disturbed'. Option (a) changes the sentence structure entirely and loses the third-person framing. Option (c) is ungrammatical, since 'let' cannot remain inside a 'that'-clause in reported speech. Option (d) uses 'ordered', which is too strong and inaccurate for what is clearly advice rather than a command, and it also drops the required 'that'-clause structure.

options: She said let him go.
She suggested him to go.
She ordered to let him go.
She suggested that he should be allowed to go.
explanation: 'Let him go' specifically expresses granting permission, and this meaning of 'let' is converted using the special pattern: 'suggested that + subject + should be allowed to + verb' — since 'let' here means 'permit', the indirect version must explicitly express permission with 'be allowed to'. Option (a) makes no transformation at all. Option (b) incorrectly uses 'suggested him to go', which is not standard grammar ('suggest' cannot take an object plus to-infinitive). Option (c) is also ungrammatical, mixing 'ordered' with 'to let', which fails to capture the permission-granting sense of the original.

set4.word: Mr. Collin said, "Let me deliver the letters to the postman."
options: Mr. Collin said that let he be allowed to deliver the letters to the postman.
Mr. Collin requested that he might be allowed to deliver the letters to the postman.
Mr. Collin suggested with joy that he wished to deliver the letters to the postman.
Mr. Collin asked if he was allowed to deliver the letters to the postman.
explanation: 'Let me...' (the speaker asking permission for themselves) is different from 'Let him...' (permission for a third person) — it is converted using the pattern: 'requested that + subject + might be allowed to + verb'. Here the reporting verb becomes 'requested' (since the speaker is asking for permission for himself), the pronoun 'me' becomes 'he' (Mr. Collin, now spoken about in third person), and 'might' — not 'should' — is used, since this expresses possibility/permission rather than an order. Option (a) incorrectly keeps 'let' inside the 'that'-clause, which is ungrammatical. Option (c) invents a meaning ('wished to') not present in the original. Option (d) turns it into a question ('asked if'), when the original was a self-permission request, not a question directed at someone else.

set4.word: The solicitor said, "Let us solve the dispute over the land."
options: wished that they should solve...
suggested that they might solve...
suggested that they should solve...
ordered that we should solve...
explanation: This is the standard 'Let us' pattern seen earlier in this set: 'suggested that + subject + should + verb'. Since the solicitor is speaking about himself and another party together ('us'), and this is now being reported by someone else, 'us' becomes 'they' in the indirect version. Option (a) uses the wrong reporting verb 'wished' (not appropriate for a practical proposal like this). Option (b) uses 'might' instead of 'should' — 'might' expresses only weak possibility, not the recommendation that 'let us' conveys. Option (d) both uses too forceful a reporting verb ('ordered') and incorrectly keeps the pronoun 'we' instead of changing it to 'they'.

set4.word: Mrs. Lisa suggested that we should purchase the property. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "Let us purchase the property."
"Property must be purchased by us."
"You may purchase the property."
"Please purchase the property."
explanation: This reverse question confirms the fixed two-way relationship between 'Let us' and 'suggested that...should': whenever 'suggested that + subject + should' appears in indirect speech, it should always be converted back to 'Let us...' in Direct Speech, and vice versa — this is a fixed, memorable pair rather than a literal word-for-word translation. Option (b) changes the meaning to a passive obligation statement, which does not match the original suggestion-style sentence. Option (c) turns it into permission-granting ('may'), a different meaning from proposing an action together. Option (d) turns it into a polite request ('Please'), which would correctly reverse to 'requested that...should', not 'suggested that...should' — a different reporting verb requires a different Direct Speech form.

options: Rita said she was ruined.
Rita exclaimed with sorrow that she was ruined.
Rita said that she ruined.
Rita exclaimed sadly that I am ruined.
explanation: Exclamatory sentences (marked by interjections like 'Alas!', 'Hurrah!', 'Oh!') follow a special conversion pattern: the interjection is removed, the reporting verb becomes 'exclaimed with [emotion]' (matching the feeling the interjection conveys — here 'Alas!' signals sorrow), and the rest of the sentence becomes a normal statement with the usual tense-backshift and pronoun changes ('am' to 'was', 'I' to 'she'). Option (a) uses plain 'said' instead of the emotion-specific 'exclaimed with sorrow', losing the exclamatory tone. Option (c) incorrectly drops the verb 'ruined'. Option (d) correctly identifies the emotion but fails to change the pronoun and tense.

set5.word: My little sister said, "What a beautiful sight!"
options: My little sister exclaimed that it was a beautiful sight.
My little sister said that it is a beautiful sight.
My little sister said that it had been a beautiful sight.
My little sister said that it would be a beautiful sight.
explanation: 'What a + adjective + noun!' is a standard exclamatory pattern showing admiration. In indirect speech it converts to: 'exclaimed that it was + adjective + noun', removing the 'What a...!' structure and replacing 'said' with the exclamation-specific 'exclaimed', along with a normal tense backshift of the implied 'is' to 'was'. Options (b), (c) and (d) all fail to use 'exclaimed' (keeping plain 'said'), missing the exclamatory tone entirely, and each applies the wrong tense — option (b) does not backshift at all, option (c) over-backshifts to Past Perfect, and option (d) wrongly turns it into a future statement.

set5.word: "Oh, how could I ever thank you, Sir!" said the convict to the Bishop.
options: The convict exclaimed gratefully to the Bishop that how he could ever thank him.
The convict said to the Bishop that he could never thank him enough.
Exclaiming gratefully, the convict said to the Bishop that he could never thank him enough.
The convict told the Bishop that it was difficult for him to thank him.
explanation: This is a more advanced exclamatory pattern where 'How could I ever...!' (a rhetorical exclamation of overwhelming gratitude, not a literal question) is best expressed using a participle phrase, 'Exclaiming gratefully', at the start, combined with a rewritten statement capturing the same meaning: 'he could never thank him enough' — an idiomatic way of expressing extreme gratitude. This shows that Narration sometimes requires natural rewording rather than a mechanical word-for-word conversion. Option (a) keeps 'how' awkwardly inside the reported clause, mixing question and statement structure. Option (b) captures the right meaning but is missing the 'Exclaiming gratefully' element reflecting the emotion of 'Oh,...!'. Option (d) shifts the meaning to 'difficulty' rather than 'gratitude', missing the actual sense of the exclamation.

set5.word: She said, "How beautiful is the rain!"
options: exclaimed with joy that the rain had been beautiful.
exclaimed with joy that the rain has been beautiful.
exclaimed with joy that the rain is beautiful.
exclaimed with joy that the rain was beautiful.
explanation: 'How + adjective + is/are + subject!' is another common exclamatory pattern expressing wonder about a quality, and it converts to: 'exclaimed with joy that + subject + was + adjective', with the 'How...!' structure removed and the reporting verb correctly changed from 'said' to 'exclaimed with joy'. The Present Tense 'is' backshifts normally to 'was', just like in any ordinary statement — exclamatory sentences follow the normal tense-backshift rules for their underlying verb, in addition to needing the emotional reporting verb and restructuring. Option (a) over-backshifts to Past Perfect, which is not needed here. Options (b) and (c) both fail to backshift the tense at all, keeping 'is' instead of 'was'.

set5.word: He exclaimed that he was a very stupid fellow. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "How a stupid fellow are you!"
"What a stupid fellow you are!"
"Are you a stupid fellow!"
"How a stupid fellow you are!"
explanation: This reverse question tests the correct grammatical construction of an exclamatory sentence containing a noun ('fellow'). When exclaiming about a noun, English always uses 'What a + adjective + noun + subject + verb!' — never 'How a...!'. 'How' is reserved for exclaiming about adjectives or adverbs alone, without a following noun (e.g. 'How beautiful!', 'How fast he runs!'), while 'What' is used whenever a noun follows the adjective (e.g. 'What a beautiful house!'). Since 'fellow' is a noun here, only 'What a stupid fellow you are!' is grammatically correct. Options (a) and (d) both incorrectly use 'How' with a following noun, a very commonly tested error. Option (c) drops the exclamatory structure entirely and turns it into a plain question.

set5.word: The professor said that had he known earlier, he would have made the necessary arrangements. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "If I knew earlier, I would have made the necessary arrangements."
"Had I known earlier, I would have made the necessary arrangements."
"If I would have known earlier, I would make arrangements."
"Had I knew earlier, I would arrange things."
explanation: This tests Third Conditional sentences (used for unreal, hypothetical past situations, often expressing regret) — the structure 'Had + subject + past participle, subject + would have + past participle' is a fixed grammatical unit that does not change its internal tense structure when converted between direct and indirect speech; only the pronoun changes ('he' to 'I', since the professor is now speaking about himself). Option (a) mismatches the tenses — 'If I knew' (a Second Conditional structure) does not correctly pair with 'would have made' (a Third Conditional consequence); mixing conditional types this way is grammatically wrong. Option (c) is doubly wrong, combining 'If' with an incorrect if-clause form and weakening the result clause. Option (d) uses the ungrammatical 'Had I knew' (should be 'known', the past participle, not 'knew').

set5.word: He observed that, had market liquidity vanished entirely, the exchange would have suspended trading. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "If market liquidity vanishes entirely, the exchange suspends trading."
"Had market liquidity vanished entirely, the exchange would have suspended trading."
"The exchange will suspend trading if market liquidity had vanished entirely."
"If the exchange suspended trading, it was because market liquidity vanished entirely."
explanation: This reinforces the same Third Conditional rule seen in the previous question: the complete 'Had + subject + past participle, subject + would have + past participle' structure must be reproduced exactly, without altering any of its internal tenses, since it already expresses a complete hypothetical past scenario (something that did not actually happen). Option (a) mistakenly converts it into a First Conditional (Present Simple plus Present Simple), changing the meaning from 'an unreal past possibility' to 'a real, general possibility' — a completely different type of conditional. Option (c) awkwardly mixes future tense with past perfect in a nonsensical combination. Option (d) restructures the sentence into a cause-and-effect explanation rather than preserving the original Third Conditional form.

set5.word: She noted that, had the literary trope not been overused, the metaphor might still have resonated with the audience. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "If the literary trope was not overused, the metaphor may still resonate with the audience."
"Had the literary trope not been overused, the metaphor might still have resonated with the audience."
"Had the literary trope not been overused, the metaphor must still resonate with the audience."
"If the literary trope had not been overused, the metaphor will still resonate with the audience."
explanation: Building on the previous two questions, this additionally tests that the specific modal used inside a Third Conditional ('might have' here, expressing a weaker, more tentative possibility than 'would have') must also remain unchanged during conversion. 'Might have resonated' cannot be swapped for a different modal like 'must' (implying certainty, a completely different meaning) or 'may' (a present-tense modal, inconsistent with the past hypothetical structure). Option (a) both weakens the conditional type and changes 'might have' to 'may'. Option (c) wrongly substitutes 'must' for 'might', turning uncertainty into near-certainty. Option (d) mismatches a correct Third Conditional if-clause with a First-Conditional-style result clause, an inconsistent hybrid.

set5.word: He asserted that the cryptographic protocol might have resisted the attack had entropy been truly random. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "Had entropy been truly random, the cryptographic protocol might have resisted the attack."
"If entropy is truly random, protocol might resist attack."
"If entropy had been random, protocol might resist attack."
"Entropy random, protocol resists attack."
explanation: This question shows that the two clauses of a conditional sentence can appear in either order (the 'had'-clause first, or the main result-clause first) without affecting the meaning or tense structure, as long as the internal tenses within each clause stay exactly as in the Third Conditional pattern. The correct answer simply reorders the clauses while preserving every tense correctly. Options (b) and (c) both incorrectly simplify the tense structure instead of using the full Third Conditional form. Option (d) drops essential grammar entirely, becoming an incomplete fragment rather than a properly formed sentence.

set5.word: He confessed that the violin concerto would have remained fragmentary had the late maestro not bequeathed his annotated sketches. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "The violin concerto would have remained fragmentary had the late maestro not bequeathed his annotated sketches."
"...if the late maestro not bequeathed his annotated sketches."
"...will be remaining fragmentary had the late maestro not bequeathed..."
"...would be fragmentary if sketches had not been bequeathed."
explanation: This final conditional question in the set confirms that the exact wording of a correctly-formed Third Conditional sentence — 'would have + past participle...had + subject + not + past participle' — must be reproduced precisely in Direct Speech, without alteration. Option (b) is grammatically broken, dropping the essential auxiliary 'had' from 'if the late maestro not bequeathed', which cannot form a valid conditional clause on its own (it needs either 'had...not bequeathed' or 'did not bequeath'). Option (c) wrongly uses the Present Continuous 'will be remaining' instead of 'would have remained', breaking the Third Conditional pattern entirely. Option (d) changes both the modal strength and the conditional clause structure, producing a weaker, inconsistent conditional type.

set6.word: The zoologist warned that the island's carrying capacity might be exceeded should invasive species proliferate unchecked. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "The island's carrying capacity will be exceeded if invasive species proliferate unchecked."
"Should invasive species have proliferated unchecked, the island's carrying capacity would be exceeded."
"The island's carrying capacity might be exceeded should invasive species proliferate unchecked."
"If invasive species proliferated unchecked, the carrying capacity had been exceeded."
explanation: 'Should + subject + base verb' (as in 'should invasive species proliferate') is an inverted, more formal way of writing an if-clause — it means exactly the same as 'if invasive species proliferate', just without the word 'if', with 'should' placed at the front and the subject and verb inverted. This inverted structure is a fixed alternative form and must be reproduced exactly, not rewritten using 'if'. Option (a) incorrectly converts it into an 'if'-clause and also wrongly strengthens the modal 'might' to 'will', changing the degree of certainty. Option (b) incorrectly adds 'have' ('should...have proliferated'), turning it into a Third Conditional past-hypothetical structure, when the original describes a general future possibility. Option (d) both converts to 'if' unnecessarily and produces a mismatched tense combination.

set6.word: She indicated that, unless the quorum were met promptly, the charter amendments would lapse. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "Unless the quorum had been met promptly, the charter amendments would have lapsed."
"Unless the quorum is met promptly, the charter amendments will lapse."
"If the quorum was not met promptly, the charter amendments might have lapsed."
"If the quorum is promptly met, the charter amendments must not lapse."
explanation: This tests an important special case: when a conditional sentence expresses a general policy, rule, or standing procedure (rather than a one-time hypothetical event), it is treated similarly to a Universal Truth and uses the Present Tense in its Direct form, even though the indirect version uses a past-style subjunctive ('were met', 'would lapse'). The correct answer restores the Present and Future structure appropriate for a standing organizational rule: 'unless the quorum is met...the amendments will lapse'. Option (a) wrongly turns it into a Third Conditional (past-hypothetical) structure, which does not fit a general, recurring rule. Option (c) unnecessarily replaces 'unless' with 'if...not' and weakens the certainty with 'might have lapsed'. Option (d) changes the meaning entirely by adding an obligation ('must not lapse') not present in the original.

set6.word: The lecturer argued that, given sufficient entropic isolation, the reaction rate could asymptotically approach zero. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "Given sufficient entropic isolation, the reaction rate could asymptotically approach zero."
"If sufficient entropic isolation was given, the reaction rate will asymptotically approach zero."
"Given sufficient entropic isolation, the reaction rate can asymptotically approach zero."
"If the reaction rate approached zero, it must have had sufficient entropic isolation."
explanation: This question checks whether students can distinguish 'could' (used here for a real, present-tense theoretical possibility, similar to but slightly softer than 'can', common in academic writing) from 'can' — since the indirect sentence already uses 'could', not 'can', the Direct Speech version must keep 'could' exactly as it is. Also, 'given sufficient entropic isolation' (meaning 'if there is/were sufficient entropic isolation') is fixed academic phrasing that should not be converted into an 'if'-clause. Option (b) incorrectly turns this into a mismatched 'if'-clause. Option (c) wrongly swaps 'could' for 'can', changing the intended nuance. Option (d) drastically restructures the sentence into a reversed cause-effect statement that does not match the original meaning.

set6.word: The analyst said that were the policies amended earlier, the crisis could have been avoided. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "If the policies were amended earlier, the crisis could have been avoided."
"Had the policies been amended earlier, the crisis could have been avoided."
"If only the policies would be amended, the crisis was avoided."
"Had we amended the policies, we avoided the crisis."
explanation: This tests a subtle distinction between two inverted conditional forms: 'Were + subject + base verb' (Second Conditional — a present or general hypothetical, meaning 'If the policies were amended') differs from 'Had + subject + past participle' (Third Conditional — a past hypothetical, meaning 'If the policies had been amended'). Since the original indirect sentence specifically uses 'were the policies amended' (not 'had the policies been amended'), the correct Direct Speech restores 'If the policies were amended earlier...', not the 'Had'-form, even though both look superficially similar. This is a commonly confused pair in advanced Narration questions — always check carefully which inverted form appears before converting. Option (c) breaks the conditional structure with 'would be amended' instead of the correct past form, and option (d) both changes the pronoun incorrectly and drops the necessary 'could have been' structure.

set6.word: He said that it would be fun if I could join. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "It will be fun if you can join."
"It will have been fun if you had joined."
"It will have been fun if I could have joined."
"It will have been fun if I had joined."
explanation: This reverse question undoes a First Conditional-style backshift: 'would be' reverses to 'will be', and the modal inside the if-clause, 'could' (the backshifted form of 'can'), reverses back to 'can' — the modal inside an if-clause is reversed along with the main clause, in the same direction. Also, since the person originally spoken to ('I' in the indirect sentence, referring to the listener) is now being addressed directly, 'I' becomes 'you' in Direct Speech. Options (b), (c) and (d) all incorrectly use the more complex 'will have been...had joined' structure, wrongly treating this as a Third Conditional (past-hypothetical) sentence, when the original 'would be fun if I could join' is actually a simpler conditional about a present or future possibility, not a completed past event.

set6.word: The ethicist argued that, absent informed consent, the study's methodology was fundamentally untenable. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "Absent informed consent, the study's methodology is fundamentally untenable."
"If the study lacked informed consent, its methodology had been fundamentally untenable."
"Without informed consent, the study's methodology would have been fundamentally untenable."
"The study's methodology must have been untenable since it lacked informed consent."
explanation: 'Absent X' is a formal academic phrase meaning 'without X' or 'in the absence of X', and when used to state a general principle (as here — an ethical/methodological principle about research, not a one-time event), the sentence is treated like a Universal Truth and its Present Tense is restored in Direct Speech, even though the indirect version shows 'was' due to the normal backshift from a past reporting verb. Option (b) wrongly restructures the phrase into an 'if'-clause and creates a mismatched, ungrammatical tense combination. Option (c) turns the general principle into a Third-Conditional-like hypothetical ('would have been'), removing its 'general truth' quality. Option (d) restructures the sentence into a different logical form (a deduction, 'must have been'), changing what kind of statement this originally was.

set6.word: The manager informed the employees that the deadline for submission had been extended by two days. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "You may submit the work two days later than planned," the manager informed.
"The deadline has been extended by two days," said the manager.
"We are extending the submission deadline by two days," the manager said.
"You must finish it within two extra days," the manager said.
explanation: This reverse question undoes the Past Perfect Passive to Present Perfect Passive backshift: 'had been extended' (Past Perfect Passive, used because 'informed' is past) reverses to 'has been extended' (Present Perfect Passive) in Direct Speech, since the manager is making a fresh, current announcement about something that has just recently happened. Options (a) and (d) both reword the sentence into a different structure (permission or obligation) rather than accurately reflecting the original announcement. Option (c) incorrectly uses Present Continuous ('are extending'), suggesting the extension is still being decided right now, rather than something already completed — a subtle but important difference from 'has been extended'.

set6.word: The envoy reported that the negotiations were languishing because neither party would concede procedural precedence. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "The negotiations languish because neither party would concede procedural precedence."
"The negotiations are languishing because neither party will be conceding procedural precedence."
"The negotiations are languishing because neither party will concede procedural precedence."
"Negotiations languished, no party conceding precedence."
explanation: This reverse question requires two separate corrections: Past Continuous 'were languishing' reverses to Present Continuous 'are languishing' (describing an ongoing, current situation at the time of the original statement), and the modal 'would' reverses to 'will' (Simple Future, not continuous). Option (a) fails to reverse the continuous tense at all, keeping bare Simple Present 'languish'. Option (b) incorrectly uses 'will be conceding' (Future Continuous), when the correct reversal of 'would concede' (Simple Conditional) is simply 'will concede' (Simple Future) — no continuous aspect was originally present. Option (d) drops proper sentence structure and tense entirely, becoming an incomplete, non-standard fragment.

set6.word: The curator noted that the pigments were deteriorating faster than the varnish could protect them. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "The pigments are deteriorating faster than the varnish can protect them."
"The pigments were deteriorating faster than the varnish can be protected by them."
"Pigments deteriorate faster than varnish protects."
"Pigment deterioration faster than varnish can protect."
explanation: Similar to the previous question, this reverses two elements together: Past Continuous 'were deteriorating' reverses to Present Continuous 'are deteriorating' (an ongoing observed process), and the modal 'could' reverses to 'can' (Simple Present ability or possibility). Option (b) confusingly switches to passive voice ('can be protected by them'), reversing the roles of subject and object and changing the meaning — it should stay active, 'the varnish can protect them', not passive. Option (c) drops the continuous aspect and the modal entirely, oversimplifying into a generic present statement that does not match the original observation. Option (d) is grammatically broken, missing a proper verb and complete sentence structure.

set6.word: The botanist sighed that the rare orchid had germinated, only to succumb to a latent fungal blight. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "The rare orchid did germinate, only to succumb to a latent fungal blight."
"A rare orchid had germinated only to succumb to a latent fungal blight."
"The rare orchid germinated, only to succumb to a latent fungal blight."
"Rare orchid germinates but succumbs to fungus."
explanation: This reverses Past Perfect back to Simple Past: 'had germinated' reverses to plain 'germinated', since Past Perfect in Indirect Speech (used because the reporting verb 'sighed' is past) simply reflects the ordinary Simple Past of the original quoted sentence. Option (a) incorrectly uses the emphatic 'did germinate' construction, which would only be used for special emphasis or contrast (e.g., 'It DID germinate, despite the odds') — not the neutral, default reversal needed here. Option (b) wrongly changes 'the rare orchid' (a specific orchid already known) to 'a rare orchid' (introducing it as new/unspecified), altering the article and the implied context. Option (d) uses Present Tense throughout, losing the past, completed nature of the event the botanist was describing.

set7.word: The historian observed that the records had been tampered with. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "Someone tampered with the records."
"The records had been tampered with."
"The records are tampered with."
"The records are fake."
explanation: This question demonstrates an important exception: Past Perfect is not always reversed to a simpler tense in Direct Speech — sometimes the original quoted sentence was already in Past Perfect, especially when the action being described happened clearly before another past reference point. In such cases, Direct Speech retains 'had been tampered with' exactly as it appears in the Indirect version, rather than automatically simplifying it. Option (a) completely changes the sentence structure from passive to active, changing the meaning. Option (c) wrongly shifts to Present Tense, losing the past context entirely. Option (d) rewrites the meaning altogether — 'are fake' is not equivalent to 'had been tampered with'.

set7.word: He commented that the policy's efficacy would remain conjectural until longitudinal data accrued. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "The efficacy of the policy remains conjectural as the data are yet to be collected."
"The policy's efficacy will remain conjectural until longitudinal data accrue."
"Until longitudinal data had accrued, the policy's efficacy might have remained conjectural."
"Efficacy remains conjecture till data accumulates."
explanation: This tests two combined rules: 'would' reverses to 'will' (Simple Future), and the verb inside a time-clause introduced by 'until' ('accrued', functioning here as a subjunctive, timeless form typical of formal future time-clauses) reverses to its base/present form 'accrue'. This reflects the general English rule that future time-clauses beginning with words like 'until', 'when', 'before', 'after', and 'as soon as' never use 'will' or a future form inside them — they use a present-tense verb even though the overall sentence refers to future time. Option (a) is a paraphrase that loses the 'will remain...until' future time-clause pattern. Option (c) incorrectly treats this as a past-hypothetical, Third-Conditional-like sentence, which does not match the original future-oriented meaning. Option (d) is an oversimplified paraphrase that changes the wording and structure significantly.

set7.word: The mentor noted that the fellow had persevered even after his hypotheses were repeatedly falsified. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "The fellow persevered even after your hypotheses were repeatedly falsified."
"The fellow persevered even after his hypotheses were repeatedly falsified."
"The fellow preserve though hypotheses are falsified."
"The fellow had persevered though his hypotheses were repeatedly falsified."
explanation: Here, 'had persevered' reverses to Simple Past 'persevered', because both actions in the sentence (persevering, and the hypotheses being falsified) occurred within the same general past timeframe as part of a connected narrative, without one clearly happening much earlier than the other — so no extra Past Perfect layer is needed in the direct quote. The pronoun 'his' stays 'his' in both versions since the mentor is speaking about a third person (the fellow) throughout, with no shift in perspective involved. Option (a) incorrectly changes 'his' to 'your', which would only be correct if the mentor were speaking directly to the fellow, but the context shows he is describing the fellow to someone else. Option (c) breaks basic subject-verb agreement and inconsistently mixes tenses. Option (d) unnecessarily keeps 'had persevered' in Past Perfect, which is not needed once reversed to Direct Speech.

set7.word: The patient said, "It has been three days since I last slept."
options: The patient said that it was three days since he last slept.
The patient said that it had been three days since he had last slept.
The patient said that he didn't sleep for three days.
The patient said that he was not sleeping for three days.
explanation: This sentence requires backshifting in two separate places within the same clause: the main verb 'has been' (Present Perfect) backshifts to 'had been' (Past Perfect), and the verb inside the 'since'-clause, 'last slept' (Simple Past), also backshifts further to 'had last slept' (Past Perfect) — both parts must shift together, since both were originally anchored to the present moment of speaking. Option (a) only backshifts the first verb and misses backshifting the second verb inside the since-clause. Options (c) and (d) both abandon the original since-clause structure and reword the meaning, losing the precise 'three days since...' time relationship the original sentence expresses.

set7.word: "I've just bought a car," said Amar, "But it isn't insured yet. So I cannot take you for a ride."
options: ...he just bought a car but it wasn't insured till then. So, he could not take me for a ride.
...he had just bought a car but it wasn't insured till then. So, he could not be taken for a ride.
...he had just bought the car but it wasn't insured till then. So, he could not take me for a ride.
...he had just bought a car but it wasn't insured till then. So, he could not take me for a ride.
explanation: This multi-sentence quote requires several coordinated changes applied consistently: 'have just bought' (Present Perfect) backshifts to 'had just bought' (Past Perfect); 'isn't' (Simple Present negative) backshifts to 'wasn't' (Simple Past negative); 'yet' changes to 'till then'; and 'cannot' backshifts to 'could not'. Importantly, the article stays 'a car', not 'the car' — because 'a car' was how it was first introduced (as something new/unspecified), and this should not change just because we are reporting it later, since that would wrongly imply the listener already knew which specific car was meant. Option (a) fails to backshift 'just bought' to Past Perfect. Option (b) wrongly turns 'take me' (active) into a passive 'be taken', changing who performs the action. Option (c) makes the same article mistake, using 'the car' instead of 'a car'.

set7.word: She said to me, "I was thinking of going out for dinner but had changed my mind later on."
options: ...she was thinking of going out for dinner but changed her mind later on.
...she thought of going out for dinner but changed her mind later on.
I was thinking of going out for dinner but changed my mind later on.
...she had been thinking of going out for dinner but changed her mind later on.
explanation: Past Continuous 'was thinking' shifts one further step back to Past Perfect Continuous 'had been thinking', since it was already in a past tense within the original quote — the same one-step-further pattern seen with other Past Continuous originals. The second verb, 'had changed', is trickier: although a standalone Past Perfect clause normally stays unchanged under backshift (as seen in earlier questions), when the direct quote already links two actions with 'but' as a mini past narrative — an intention followed by a later, completed change of mind — the official answer here treats plain Simple Past 'changed' as the correct final form rather than doubling the backshift further. This is a genuinely tricky, atypical case worth remembering exactly as tested, since it differs from the usual 'Past Perfect stays unchanged' pattern seen with an isolated Past Perfect clause. Options (a) and (c) fail to backshift 'was thinking' to the fuller Past Perfect Continuous form, and option (b) drops the continuous sense of the original intention entirely.

set7.word: Hari said to the boy, "What's the matter? Why are you crying? Be cheerful."
options: Hari asked the boy what the matter was and why he was crying. He asked him to be cheerful.
Hari asks the boy what the matter was and why he was crying. He asks him to be cheerful.
Hari asked the boy what the matter was and why he was crying. He asks him to be cheerful.
Hari asked the boy what was the matter and he was crying. He asked him to be cheerful.
explanation: This sentence combines three different sentence types in one quote — two Wh-questions ('What's the matter?', 'Why are you crying?') and one Imperative ('Be cheerful') — and each must be converted according to its own rule, while keeping the reporting verbs consistently in the past tense throughout. The two Wh-questions use 'asked' plus normal statement word order: 'what the matter was' and 'why he was crying'. The imperative 'Be cheerful' converts to 'asked him to be cheerful' (object plus to plus base verb). Options (b) and (c) incorrectly switch to present-tense reporting verbs partway through, creating an inconsistent tense within a single passage — once you start reporting in the past, you must stay consistent throughout. Option (d) wrongly keeps the inverted question word order 'what was the matter'.

set7.word: The teacher paused and said, "Those who are tired of listening to me can go. I shall not mind it."
options: ...ordered that those who were tired of listening to him would go and that he would not mind that.
...paused and then said that those who have been tired of listening to him would go...
...exclaimed with a pause and then said that those who are tired of listening to me can go...
...paused and said that those who were tired of listening to him could go and that he would not mind that.
explanation: This sentence contains two separate but related statements that must be combined smoothly into one connected Indirect Speech sentence, joined using 'that' as a connector: 'are tired' backshifts to 'were tired', the modal 'can' backshifts to 'could' (permission being reported, not obligation), and 'I shall not mind' backshifts to 'he would not mind' with the correct first-to-third-person pronoun change. Option (a) incorrectly uses 'ordered' as the reporting verb, when the original tone is permissive rather than a command, and it wrongly backshifts 'can' all the way to 'would' instead of 'could'. Option (b) fails to backshift the tense correctly and does not backshift 'can' to 'could' at all. Option (c) fails to backshift any of the tenses or pronouns, essentially leaving the sentence almost unchanged, which is incorrect since a past reporting verb requires backshifting.

set7.word: He ordered his soldiers to take that bird away. Its gilded cage reminded him of his father whom he had imprisoned. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "May this bird be taken away. Its gilded cage reminds me of my father whom I imprisoned."
"Take the bird away. Its gilded cage reminded him of his father whom I imprison."
"Take this bird away. Its gilded cage reminded me of my father whom I imprison."
"Take this bird away. Its gilded cage reminds me of my father whom I imprisoned."
explanation: This reverse question requires careful, separate treatment of each part of the sentence. The imperative 'ordered...to take' reverses to a plain command, 'Take', with no auxiliary needed. 'That bird' (distant, as referred to by the reporter) reverses to 'this bird' (near, as the speaker himself would refer to it while looking at it directly). 'Reminded' (Simple Past, describing his ongoing feeling as reported in the past) reverses to 'reminds' (Present Tense), reflecting a present, ongoing emotional reaction at the moment of speaking rather than a one-time past event. Finally, 'had imprisoned' (Past Perfect, describing a single, clearly-completed past action before the 'reminding' moment) stays as 'imprisoned' (Simple Past) since it refers to a genuinely completed past event needing no further reversal. Options (a) and (c) incorrectly use present-tense 'imprison' for a clearly completed past action. Option (b) wrongly leaves 'reminded' unreversed and uses 'him' instead of the correct first-person 'me'.

set7.word: "Dear daughter," she said caressing her cheeks, "have you come to comfort me in my time of need?"
options: Caressing her daughter's cheeks, she asked affectionately if she had come to comfort her in her time of need.
...she said affectionately if she had been coming to comfort her in her time of need.
...she asked affectionately if she did come to comfort her in her time of need.
...she said affectionately if she has come to comfort her in her time of need.
explanation: This sentence tests several advanced points together: the direct address 'Dear daughter' is simply deleted in Indirect Speech (terms of address are not reported literally), while the participle phrase 'Caressing her cheeks' (describing an action happening alongside the speech) is retained and simply repositioned, with 'her' now clearly referring back to 'her daughter's cheeks'. Since the direct sentence is a Yes/No question, the reporting verb correctly becomes 'asked' (not 'said') — and since it carries a warm, loving tone, 'affectionately' reflects that. Finally, Present Perfect 'have you come' backshifts to Past Perfect 'had she come', with normal statement word order (no inversion). Option (b) incorrectly uses 'said' instead of 'asked' and wrongly changes the tense to Past Perfect Continuous. Option (c) also wrongly uses 'said' and incorrectly inserts 'did come' instead of the correct Past Perfect. Option (d) uses 'said' instead of 'asked' and fails to backshift the tense at all.

set8.word: You said, "Priya kept watching television till her mother exploded at her."
options: You said that Priya had kept watching television till her mother exploded at her.
You said that Priya kept watching television till her mother had exploded at her.
You said that Priya kept watching television till her mother exploded at her.
You said that Priya had kept watching television till her mother had exploded at her.
explanation: This question demonstrates an important exception to the normal backshift rule: when the direct speech quote is itself already a complete, self-contained narrative describing a sequence of past events (here, connected by 'till', showing one action continuing until another happened), and both actions are already clearly in Simple Past showing their own internal sequence, no further backshift into Past Perfect is applied — the Simple Past tenses are left exactly as they are, because backshifting them further would distort the clear 'till' sequence relationship between the two actions. Options (a), (b) and (d) all incorrectly add extra Past Perfect backshifting to one or both verbs, which is unnecessary and would confuse the timeline, since the sentence already tells a complete past story with its own internal sequencing via 'till'.

set8.word: Isha told Shivani that they didn't need to buy tickets for the match as she had been given free passes for two. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "We didn't need to buy tickets for the match as I am given free passes for two."
"We don't need to buy tickets for the match as I have been given free passes for two."
"They didn't need to buy tickets for the match as she was given free passes for two."
"They didn't need to buy tickets for the match as I am given free passes for two."
explanation: This challenging reverse question requires three coordinated reversals: the pronoun 'they' (referring to Isha and Shivani together, as seen by an outside reporter) reverses to 'we' (since Isha is now speaking about herself and Shivani together, in her own direct words); 'didn't need' (Simple Past negative) reverses to 'don't need' (Simple Present negative); and 'had been given' (Past Perfect Passive) reverses to 'have been given' (Present Perfect Passive). Option (a) correctly reverses the pronoun and negative tense but incorrectly uses 'am given' (Simple Present Passive) instead of 'have been given' (Present Perfect Passive). Options (c) and (d) incorrectly keep 'they' instead of changing it to 'we', which would only be correct if someone other than Isha herself were speaking these words directly.

set8.word: I told you that I had bought a new book for you the day before. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "I have bought a new book for you the day before."
"I bought a new book for you yesterday."
"You have bought a new book for me the day before."
"I told to you, 'I have bought a new book for you yesterday.'"
explanation: This reverse question requires two changes: 'had bought' (Past Perfect) reverses to 'have bought' (Present Perfect), and 'the day before' (a phrase only appropriate when reporting something after the fact) reverses to 'yesterday' (appropriate when the statement was originally made just one day after the event). Although the phrasing 'I told to you' in the correct answer is somewhat unusual (the more standard form would simply be 'I told you'), it is the only option that correctly performs both necessary reversals together — a reminder that in these multiple-choice questions, priority should go to whichever option gets the core tense and time-word reversal completely right, even if its surrounding phrasing is not perfectly polished. Option (a) fails to reverse 'the day before' to 'yesterday'. Option (b) correctly reverses both elements but does not frame it as a properly reported statement. Option (c) incorrectly swaps the pronouns 'I' and 'you', reversing who bought the book for whom.

set8.word: Your sister will say that she has lost her pen again. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "I lost my pen again."
"She has lost my pen again."
"I lost her pen again."
"I have lost my pen again."
explanation: Since the reporting verb here is in the future tense ('will say', describing something someone is expected to say later, rather than something already said), the tense of the reported clause is not backshifted at all in the original indirect sentence — 'has lost' stays as Present Perfect. Therefore, reversing to Direct Speech, 'has lost' simply becomes 'have lost' (matching first person 'I', since the sister will be speaking about herself), with no further tense change needed. The pronoun 'she'/'her' reverses to 'I'/'my' since the sister is now speaking her own words directly. Options (a) and (c) incorrectly use Simple Past 'lost' instead of the correct Present Perfect 'have lost'. Option (b) fails to change 'her pen' to 'my pen' and incorrectly keeps 'has' instead of 'have' for the first-person subject.

set8.word: Mahendra told her that he would be waiting for her at the theatre. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: Mahendra told her, "He will be waiting for her at the theatre."
Mahendra asked her, "I will be waiting for you at the theatre."
Mahendra inquired her, "He will be waiting for you at the theatre."
Mahendra told her, "I will be waiting for you at the theatre."
explanation: This reverse question requires three coordinated reversals happening together: 'would' (backshifted future) reverses to 'will' (Simple Future); 'he' (third person, as spoken about by someone reporting Mahendra's words) reverses to 'I' (since Mahendra is now speaking his own words in Direct Speech); and 'her' (referring to the listener, from an outside reporter's perspective) reverses to 'you' (since Mahendra is now speaking directly to her). The reporting verb correctly stays as 'told' (not 'asked'), since the original sentence is a plain statement, not a question. Option (a) fails to reverse the pronouns. Options (b) and (c) incorrectly change the reporting verb to 'asked'/'inquired', which are only appropriate for questions, not statements.

set8.word: You told me that you had not slept the previous night and could not work then. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "I have not slept last night and could not work then."
"I did not sleep last night and cannot work now."
"I have not slept the previous night and cannot work then."
"You did not sleep the previous night and could not work now."
explanation: This is one of the most challenging reverse questions in the set, requiring four separate, coordinated changes at once: 'had not slept' (Past Perfect negative) reverses to 'did not sleep' (Simple Past negative, not Present Perfect, since the original spoken sentence would describe a specific completed night's events using Simple Past); 'could not work' reverses to 'cannot work' (modal 'could' reverses to 'can'); 'the previous night' reverses to 'last night'; and 'then' reverses to 'now'. Every time-and-tense marker must be reversed together for the sentence to be fully restored to its original spoken form. Option (a) fails to reverse 'the previous night' to 'last night' and wrongly uses 'have not slept' instead of 'did not sleep'. Option (c) makes the same tense error and also fails to reverse 'then' to 'now'. Option (d) wrongly keeps the pronoun 'You' (should be 'I') and leaves 'could not' unreversed.

set8.word: She told me that she had already finished the project. (Convert to DIRECT)
options: "She will have finished the project."
